content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 29, 2016

Thomas A. Beltran
P.O. Box 0850
Borrego Springs, CA 92004

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-16-056

Dear Mr. Beltran:

       This letter responds to your request for advice regarding your duties under the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Note that the Fair Political Practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, our advice is based solely on the provisions of the
Act. We offer no opinion on the applicability of other laws, such as common law conflict of
interest.

QUESTION

       May you participate in consideration of a General Plan Amendment (the "Amendment")
affecting property within 500 feet of a vacation home owned through your family trust?

CONCLUSION

       Based on the magnitude of the change to the property in proximity to your vacation home,
there will be a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your property and you may not make,
participate in making, or influence the decision.

FACTS

       You are the chairperson of the Borrego Springs Community Sponsor Group
(BSCSG). The BSCSG is an advisory group to the County of San Diego (San Diego County).
According to the County's website, members of the BSCSG are public officials required to file
Form 700s disclosing their financial interests.² The BSCSG will be considering and possibly voting

_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² http://www.sandiegocounty.gov/content/sdc/pds/gpupdate/comm/borrego.html, Borrego Springs Sponsor
Group FAQ.

File No. A-16-056
Page No. 2

on a Property Specific General Plan Amendment on April 7, 2016. The Amendment proposes to
rezone two projects in the BSCSG planning area (i.e. within the jurisdiction of BSCSG).

One of the projects (DS-24) proposed for rezoning property is owned by Borrego Country
Club Estates LLC consisting of 172.9 acres. DS-24's boundary is within a few hundred feet of a
vacation home owned by your family trust. The Amendment would rezone DS-24 from one
dwelling unit per 10 acres to one dwelling unit per 1 acre.

Your vacation home has been appraised two or three times in the last five years and none
considered vacant lots such as the ones proposed for rezoning in the Amendment. Since appraisal
"comparables" do not include vacant lots, you stated you are not sure if the vacation home's value
would be affected by the Amendment.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in
making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official
has a financial interest specified in Section 87103.³ A public official has a "financial interest" in a
government decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section
87103.)

Your question solely concerns real property held by your trust. Section 87103 provides in
pertinent part:

"A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning
of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on
the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on...

* * *

"(h) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect
interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more."

Moreover, under Regulation 18234(c), a public official has an economic interest in real
property held in a trust when the official is either the trustor or a beneficiary of the trust that meets

³ Regulation 18704 provides: "A public official makes a governmental decision if the official authorizes or
directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters
into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. A public official participates in a governmental decision
if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting a decision without
significant intervening substantive review. (C) A public official uses his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision if he or she: (1) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency
subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose of affecting a decision; or (2) Contacts
or appears before any official in any other government agency for the purpose of affecting a decision, and the public
official acts or purports to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact."

File No. A-16-056
Page No. 3

certain conditions. Because it is your trust that holds the property, you have an interest in your
vacation home.

Foreseeability and Materiality. Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably
foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the
official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in
the decision, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be
considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) Based upon the facts provided, your
real property interest is not explicitly involved in decisions regarding the Amendment and a
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and
more than hypothetical or theoretical.

Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. (Regulation 18702.) Regulation
18702.2 provides that any reasonably foreseeable effect on an interest in real property is material
whenever the governmental decision affects real property located within 500 feet of the property
line of the official's property, unless the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the property.
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(11).)

In this case, however, based on the size of the property subject to the decision, the
magnitude of the change to the property and the proximity of your property to the subject property,
we believe there will be a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your property. The property
proposed for rezoning is within a few hundred feet of a vacation home and is currently vacant and
developable up to one dwelling per ten acres. The decision will rezone the property from one
dwelling per 10 acres to one dwelling unit per 1 acre. This is a tenfold increase in the number of
houses that may be built in proximity to your home. While we are mindful that the actual
development of the property will not be a part of the current decision, it is certain that if the
rezoning does not occur, such an increase in development cannot not occur. Therefore, this
decision is a necessary prerequisite to development of the property at one dwelling unit per acre and
you have a conflict of interest.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

affecting property within 500 feet of a vacation home owned through your family trust?

CONCLUSION: Based on the magnitude of the change to the property in proximity to your vacation home,
